title: Baked Rice Pudding
subtitle: Arborio rice slow-baked in whole milk until thick and creamy, with a golden nutmeg skin
excerpt: The simplest of British puddings: short-grain rice and whole milk in a buttered dish, baked low and slow for two hours until the top skin turns golden and the rice beneath is thick and yielding. Serve with a spoonful of jam or a drizzle of cream.
sourceNotes: Baked rice pudding appears in Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) as one of the foundational British milk puddings. The recipe has changed very little since then: short-grain rice, whole milk, sugar, butter, and nutmeg baked at a low temperature for a long time.

content.text: Baked rice pudding asks almost nothing of the cook. Everything goes into one dish, the oven does the work, and the result is a thick, creamy pudding with a golden skin on top that people tend to argue about wanting. The skin forms naturally as the milk reduces against the heat of the dish, and it is worth having.

Short-grain rice is the right rice for this pudding. Arborio or any short-grain variety releases its starch slowly into the milk, thickening it from within. Long-grain rice gives a grainy, waterlogged result. Use whole milk, not semi-skimmed, the fat carries the flavour and helps the skin form properly.
A shallow baking dish or roasting tin, about 1.5 litres capacity. Buttering the dish helps the pudding release cleanly and adds to the flavour of the skin.

Heat the oven to 150°C fan. Butter a 1.5-litre baking dish generously. Tip {{arborio-rice}} of short-grain rice into the dish. Pour {{whole-milk}} of whole milk over, add {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar, and stir briefly to distribute. Dot {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in small pieces across the surface. Grate a generous amount of nutmeg over the top.

Bake for 30 minutes, then stir the skin back into the pudding with a spoon. Return to the oven and bake for a further 1 hour 30 minutes without stirring. The top should be golden and the pudding beneath thick and creamy when you push a spoon through the skin. If the skin is pale after 90 minutes, raise the heat to 170°C fan for the final 10 minutes.
Serve straight from the dish. A spoonful of raspberry or strawberry jam on the side is traditional. A drizzle of extra cream is good if the pudding has thickened more than you'd like.

items.fix: Raise to 170°C fan and bake for a further 20 minutes uncovered. A shallower dish spreads the milk over a wider surface and helps it reduce more quickly.
items.cause: Oven temperature was too low, or the dish was too deep so the milk didn't reduce
items.symptom: Pudding is still liquid after two hours

items.fix: Stir at the 30-minute mark to break any early skin and redistribute the rice. Make sure to use arborio or another short-grain rice, not long-grain.
items.cause: The skin formed too early and sealed in the rice before it cooked through
items.symptom: Rice is hard in the centre

items.fix: Leave the dish uncovered throughout. Raise to 170°C fan for the last 15 minutes. The skin needs direct heat to colour.
items.cause: Oven temperature too low, or the dish was covered
items.symptom: Skin is pale and soft rather than golden

items.fix: Use a deeper dish next time. Check the oven temperature with a thermometer, fan ovens can run 10, 15°C hotter than the dial reads.
items.cause: Dish was too shallow, or oven temperature was too high

content.text: Baked rice pudding is one of the longest-running items in British home cooking. It featured in the Victorian household guides of the 1860s and in countless school and hospital menus through the twentieth century. Its reputation suffered from institutional overcooking. The homemade version, baked slowly in a well-buttered dish, bears no resemblance to the over-thinned canteen one. It has been a staple of British home kitchens for as long as rice has been an affordable pantry ingredient.
